content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies where a dinner party goes wrong

**Score:** 108
**Body:** I’ve just rewatched two of my favorites; The Invitation and Coherence. Looking for similar movies. They don’t have to be thrillers/horror. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Menu
2. Clue maybe?
3. Dinner for Schmucks
4. The Ref... Works as a Christmas movie too.
5. Big Night. It’s a fun watch.
6. The Last Supper (1995)
7. The Birdcage
8. Who’s afraid of Virginia Woolf.
9. Rope.
10. The Christmas dinner episode of The Bear is the most uncomfortably realistic portrayal of a terrible family dinner.

## 2. What are some of the best "genre-parody" movies?

**Score:** 28
**Body:** Scary Movie (horror) - comedy

Last Action Hero - Action/comedy - arnold schqarzenegger

Dont be a menace to south central while drinking your juice in the hood - (crime-drama) - comedy

Austin Powers - Spy - comedy

etc;

...

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Galaxy Quest
2. Walk Hard: The Dewey Cox Story is a great parody of the late 00's biopics on Ray Charles and Johnny Cash.
3. This Is Spinal Tap


Best In Show
4. Abbott and Costello Meet Frankenstein (1948) - Universal horror movies

Airplane! (1980) - disaster movies

Blazing Saddles (1974) - westerns

The Naked Gun: From the Files of Police Squad! (1988) - police dramas

The Rutles: All You Need Is Cash (1978) - The Beatles

This Is Spinal Tap (1984) - rock music documentaries

Top Secret! (1984) - spy movies and Elvis Presley movies

Young Frankenstein (1974) - Frankenstein movies
5. Would The Cabin In The Woods be parody or deconstruction? I recommend it either way.
6. Hot Shots! and Hot Shots! Part Deux
7. Shaun of the Dead

Hot Fuzz
8. Not Another Teen Movie- teen/comedy
9. Blazing Saddles - made it impossible to take a western seriously for a while
10. The Blackening is super good!

## 3. Looking for overlooked movies that are weird, surreal, and aesthetically interesting?

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Been thinking a lot about *the Double* (2013) again. Not only was it a neat mix-and-match of genres but it was also aesthetically interesting. I’m not looking for the exact “double” 😏 with similar plot or aesthetics but rather something that stands out while often being overlooked. Something that might have gone under my radar.

If it’s well known like *Lost Highway*, *Jacob's Ladder*, *Under the Skin*, *Enter the Void*, *Stalker*, *Vivarium*, *Brazil*, *the Lobster*, etc, there’s a high chance I’ve already seen it.

What I’m trying to avoid are those artsy movies, mostly European ones, that are weird for the sake of being weird. It’s some kind of social statement or metaphor or something and it all goes right over my head. Call me dumb or whatever but I cannot figure those out. I need the surface level to be entertaining.

Any suggestions? I will put more examples below. Thanks in advance!

The movies at the top of my head that I *didn’t* enjoy are

* *Beyond the Black Rainbow* \- I wanted to like it but I have no clue what was going on.
* *The Whisperer in Darkness*
* *The Hourglass Sanatorium*
* *The Wolf House -* I admire the creativity but my mind was blank and I got bored
* *Messiah of Evil* \- I liked some parts but overall it was boring
* *Hatching*
* *Mandy*
* *Color Out of Space*
* *I'm Thinking of Ending Things -* I hate this movie
* *Last Night in Soho*
* *Melancholia -* no *Lars von Trier* in general

The ones that I enjoyed are

* *Angel's Egg*
* *In Fabric*
* *Come True*
* *Tale of Tales*
* *November*
* *The Beach House*
* *The Face of Another*
* *The Eyes of My Mother*
* *The Love Witch*
* *Possession*
* *The Neon Demon*

Oh and just to update. I'm planning to watch *Smoking Causes Coughing*.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Annihilation is a good one for that. Creepy, strange, only mildly coherent by the end, extreme visuals and designs, cool stuff.
  - Seen and loved it.
2. Tank Girl
3. Paris, Texas


It’s surreal and beautiful in just the right way, with some of the best cinematography I’ve ever seen. It’s completely overlooked and underrated by the general public. Although it often shows up on critics lists of best films of the century.
4. More mainstream but maybe not quirky enough? 

A Serious Man
5. Mr. Nobody.  I hidden and beautiful gem.
6. Dark City 
(overlooked generally but does get some love in these kind of threads)

The Delicatessen 

City of Lost Children
7. Hear me out. Secretary (2002) got 80s/90s kitsch so right. The visuals and colors were so pleasing and apt in the interior of the Law office and in Lee's inner world, all gaudy pinks, greens and blues. 
8. [The Doom Generation](https://www.rottentomatoes.com/m/doom_generation) (1995)
9. The Imaginarium of Doctor Parnassus. Can't believe it's not already on that list lol

## 4. Movies like Stand By Me

**Score:** 14
**Body:** I recently watched stand by me and loved it so much! The friendship, the adventure, the boyhood/brotherhood were all things I really loved about the movie! Do you know of any movies with similar element?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Not the same, but Goonies would make a great double feature.
2. Now and Then is kinda like the girl version of Stand By Me.
3. Check out,  The Outsiders, Rumble Fish, Holes and Super 8

Super 8 has some scify in it but it starts like Stranger Things
4. October sky HAS to be one. It's in my top 3 favorite films of all time.
5. Breaking Away
6. Moonrise Kingdom
7. Breaking Away
8. The Lost Boys
9. The Sandlot
10. It (1990)

## 5. Looking for a movie that’s perfect for a rainy day

**Score:** 12
**Body:** It’s one of those days where the rain won’t stop, and I’m just in the mood to curl up with a good movie. Not too heavy, but definitely something that leaves you feeling good. Any suggestions?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Amelie
2. I’m watching Casablanca rn. Old black n white but still a great movie.
3. Chef
4. The Holiday
5. If you enjoy older movies and a good whodunnit, Rear Window (1954) and the remake (1998) are both great. I watched one right after the other. :)
6. The Princess Bride, Stardust, Ladyhawke, The Grand Budapest Hotel, While You Were Sleeping, You’ve Got Mail, Overboard, Moonstruck
7. Singing in the rain.

Some like it hot

What’s up doc

Back to the future
8. When Harry Met Sally
9. City Island is a hidden gem if you ask me
10. Hector and the search for Happiness

Eurotrip

The Hundred foot journey

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Movie with the best shootout scene? I'll start

**Score:** 1841
**Body:** The shootout scene in Heat was highly praised by audiences as well as critics.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sicario the road into the USA scene
  - Yeah, SICARIO customs line, was one of the best. 

Other great ones (I don’t choose a favorite. Depends on my mood): 

[Children of Men - the best one shot battle scene ever. Yeah better than 1917 for me](https://youtu.be/JvPiKhuMzB0?si=sMhOQFgCHN0pvvP5) 

(Not a movie, but for TV) [Richard Harrow in Boardwalk Empire S3E12](https://youtu.be/0o5LfzKaSEM?si=vt8CjCDsU8pN0Ym4)

SPR - the defense of the bridge

Hard Boiled - tea house or the warehouse or the hospital, take your pick.
2. Way of the Gun had a fantastic final shootout scene! Best? Hard to beat Heat…
3. 3:10 to Yuma
4. Blackhawk Down?  The whole movie is literally a shoot you're way out of this city.
5. Besides Heat, The Town
6. Nobody is gonna say Wind River?
  - Stop flanking me!
  - Fuck you, let's go.
7. Collateral

## 2. You can only watch one 👀

**Score:** 884

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dune Part Three
  - Easy choice, the spice must flow
  - No contest. I’m interested in Avengers and Spider Man, I really do hope they’re good, but honestly I stopped giving a shit about Marvel a long time ago. I’m intrigued by the Odyssey simply because it’s a Christopher Nolan film, but no adaptation of the Odyssey will ever top O’ Brother, Where Art Thou?. Dune is the only one I’m genuinely hyped for.
    - O’ Brother is the GOAT for sure. 

Definitely want to see what Nolan does, but I’m not expecting anything to top that.
    - Wait a minute. How did I fucking miss O Brother being an adaption of The Odyssey
  - Where I live there's no IMAX screens available, but where my sister lives it does. 

Next year, I’m literally getting on a plane just to watch Dun...I mean to see my sister, of course. Just to see my sister.
2. My arrakis. My dune.
3. Dune
4. Dune part three, is this even a question? Cmon. Villeneuve’s closing of the trilogy
5. I will watch them all though

## 3. In Saving Private Ryan, why not have Jackson snipe the machine gunners?

**Score:** 815
**Body:** When they assault the machine gun nest whereWade gets killed, Jackson trades his sniper rifle and joins the others charging right into the gun. I’m no soldier, but that doesn’t seem like good strategy. I understand from a dramatic point of view, you don’t want to use him like a cheat code. And they needed to set up the scene after where the group starts falling apart and Miller reveals he’s a teacher. But in-universe, just let Jackson do it. He already did it before on Omaha beach

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ask Anakin what happens when you attack someone who has the high ground.
  - He's stumped.
2. Barry Pepper is pitching 137 MPH in this role.
  - Barry Pepper absolutely cooks in every film he’s in. It’s a shame that he never became a bigger star because he has the looks and acting chops to wreck whatever he plays. 

Severely underrated actor without question
    - Lucky Ned in True Grit.
    - Loved him as Roger Maris in 61*
3. “Why does the larger soldier not simply just eat the smaller one?!”
4. The machine guns were inside pillboxes if I can remember. Can’t easily hit them with a rifle from a lower position like they were in.
  - Also america didn't really have a sniper program in WWII especially early in the war, but even by DDay it wasn't a very fleshed out system and the rifles were jury normal infantry rifles with off the shelf scopes on them.
    - Yeah and snipers were pretty much boys who were really good at shooting squirrels and shit as kids.

## 4. Which movie do you wish you could watch again for the first time?

**Score:** 290

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Matrix
  - I went to see it opening weekend without knowing anything about the plot. All I knew was the title and that Keanu was in it. Greatest moviegoing experience of my life.
    - Same. My mate dragged me to it. He also dragged me to Pulp Fiction. Another mate made me see Lock, Stock and 2 Smoking barrels at the movies.
I guess I have good friends
2. The usual suspects
3. 12 monkeys
4. Snatch
Lucky number slevin
Lock and Stock

Love a good plot twist
  - Lucky number Slevin is so good
5. Yup, The Game absolutely!

But also The Prestige.  
Of which the first three or so viewings all qualify as "the first time".. 🤔
6. I know it isn't a cinematic masterpiece, but the initial 3D IMAX experience with the original Avatar is something that i don't know I'll ever experience again. To have that *moment* again would be amazing. 

I wish i could see Apocalypto again for the first time. I haven't watched it since my initial viewing, but i don't know if I'll be as tense on a second watch.

Third place is Get Out. I love the things i pick up the times i watch it again, but to have that reveal again fresh for the first time... man, that's what movies are about
7. The Matrix or Men in Black. I saw both in the theater.

## 5. Classic: Sister Act 2

**Score:** 85
**Body:** Sister Act 2 was a classic. A coming of age casting, humorous, Lauryn Hill is truly a beautiful singer. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. And immediately, "Oh happy day..." comes to mind.

Lauryn Hill and Jennifer Love Hewitt were so young.
  - 🎶Joyful, joyful🎶
  - "When Jesus WAAAAAAAASHED..."

Everyone: \*jaw drop\*
2. as a jew, Sister Act and Sister Act 2 are the only reason I know any Jesusy songs
  - As a non Jew, Curb Your Enthusiasm is why I know Jewsy songs
3. Seeing the commercial for the first time, I laid eyes on a beautiful girl and wondered "Who's she?" 

Jennifer Love Hewitt.
  - JLH, Lauryn Hill, and Roxy all before they were famous. Excellent casting.
4. She sure had a great run of movies in the 90s that appealed to 10yr old me. 


I also played the shit out of this soundtrack on cassette tape
5. The "Rappers" in the class LOL
6. Directed by Bill Duke.  Better known for playing action movie tough guys.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The "Press Junket" model is broken: Why actors switch to autopilot, and why we need to change the questions we ask.

**Score:** 64
**Body:** I’ve been reading a lot lately about actors expressing discomfort with promotional tours. Lupita Nyong’o described press junkets as "polite torture," and Cillian Murphy called the model "broken."   
  
It’s easy to look at a bored actor in an interview and blame them for being difficult, or blame the journalist for asking "shallow" questions. But I think the issue is structural. 

**The Assembly Line Problem** 

The junket is an industrial format: compressed slots, repetition, and hyper-control. When an actor answers "What drew you to the character?" for the 50th time in a row, they aren't engaging with the craft anymore; they are just performing an image. The interview ceases to be an inquiry and becomes an endurance exercise. 

As a result, audiences are losing out. We are fed anecdotes and "vibes" instead of actual insight into filmmaking. Even the "fun" alternatives (like puppy interviews or spicy wings) sidestep the craft entirely to focus on personality. 

**The Solution:** 

Technical Questions I believe that if we want to save the promotional interview, we need to stop asking emotional questions ("How did it feel?") and start asking technical ones. Questions that treat the actor as a craftsperson. 

If you ask an actor about their method, they usually wake up. 

I’ve been brainstorming what these questions should look like. Instead of the usual fluff, what if press junkets focused on things like: 

* "Was there a scene where you chose to remove an intention or a gesture rather than adding one?" 
* "In the most complex scene, did you rely on a specific method or a personal synthesis?" 
* "Looking at the finished film, what aspect of the performance required the most delicate balance to maintain?" 

These aren't flashy, but they force the actor to think about the work, not the sales pitch. 

What do you think? Do you find yourself skipping standard interviews nowadays? And are there any interviewers out there currently who you think are actually breaking this mold?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You're overlooking the economic aspect, which is why interviews happen at all.


They're not insights into the craft, they're adverts for the movie and product to sell the interviewer's magazine/channel/etc...


They ask generic questions because that's what their core audience wants.


Most people don't want highly technical movie making insights, they want fun anecdotes and easily digestible soundbites.
  - A few weeks ago David Ehrlich said he was interviewing PTA/cast and wondered if his followers had any questions they wanted answered and a guy earnestly suggested “ask what drew them to the project” like that it’s the first question of every promo interview. 

People want the dull questions.
2. The press junket is purely there to increase the general audience's interest in seeing the film. Your questions about acting methods would fall on deaf ears.

The only PR channels where method-specific questions work would be something like Inside the Actor's Studio (RIP) or Variety's Actors on Actors or the THR's Roundtable.

These are all industry-facing channels where actors feature not only for their film but for their own Awards season prospects.

The general public simply won't care to hear about an actor's process.
  - Yeah, they're advertorials and not real interviews. 
3. I think that I don’t especially care how effective marketing strategies are and want to make them better. I’m not in the marketing business, I’m in the film-liking not-business.

And I think it’s a massive mistake to think that these practices are in any way related to your or my interest in film.

The way capitalism makes consumers so invested that they search for ways to be better advertised to is frankly terrifying.

I’d rather build a better society than a better advert.
4. Not every person has a technically oriented or analytical mind. While some actors definitely do (and I want to hear the filmmaking answers!), it’s usually considered a profession that’s more about feeling than anything else. It’s our job to explain what they’re doing and how it fits into the bigger picture. I’ve seen directors look uncomfortable on Q&A panels because they’re being asked to describe something that doesn’t really exist. They’re showing you the picture, and in most cases that’s what they actually wanted to say – at least for now. (Anything else is *Brazil*.)

That said, I agree with you on the general premise – that marketing should be talent-oriented, and that we need more different stories, not just the ones already in circulation. I wrote about this [here](https://www.reddit.com/r/WesBentley/comments/1p4v82y/echoes_stories_the_actor_who_outgrew_the_plot_wes/), comparing the story told about Wes Bentley to *Hollywood Sections of the Cross*. Every time he’s interviewed, he’s asked about *American Beauty* and his addiction – things that happened fifteen or more years ago. His rarer interviews show an appreciation for cinema and genuine insight into the craft. I really want more of that, although I do understand why people like hearing the redemption arc all over again.

I’ll bring you a good story soon, though – I just need to stitch it all together. I went to a Q&A panel for *Rebuilding* (2025) with Max Walker-Silverman and asked what I assumed was a weird question (I even said that out loud into the mic – most embarrassing). Somehow, he got me and gave a perfect answer: “I don’t care about Westerns as a genre. I’m telling you the story of my town.”
5. I have always wondered just who the target audience is for these junkets. It's taken at face value that actors doing interview marathons is necessary for a movie to succeed, but... who watches them? Maybe a small audience of movie nerds and fans of that actor , both of whom are already aware of the upcoming film. Is there any segment of the population that is going to be converted from a non-watcher to a watcher based on some interview snippet that airs on [wherever these interviews even air, I don't know]?
6. I think you’re way overestimating what the Entertainment Tonight audience is looking for.  The questions could be more in depth, but the point of all these interviews is to have the actors everywhere the week or two before the film comes out to promote the film to as wide an audience as possible.  
  
These are a boring, tiring slog for actors.  They’re also negotiated into their contracts.  They are being paid for them and it’s probably a lot more than you or I get paid for a week or two of boring, tiring work.  A-listers with more pull will often negotiate less press tour time into their contracts.  
  
So yeah, actors understandably hate these, but they’re not going away unless the financial incentives for studios change.  And really, we don’t need to feel bad for Hollywood stars because every time they have a movie come out they have to fly around for a couple days and get interviewed with generic questions.  There are other formats where they can discuss their films more in depth after they’re released if that’s something they want to do.
7. people in this thread are disagreeing with you on the basis that press junkets are there to raise awareness of the movie. But looking at the box office, it seems obvious that that reasoning isn't panning out into increased moviegoers.
8. I like Variety's model where they get two actors or two directors to interview each other. It brings up questions they find interesting without being combative. Talk shows can do interesting things, too. Drew Barrymore's interview of Ryan Kiera Armstrong for Firestarter was way better than the movie they were promoting.

## 2. The Holdovers as a Stoic film — did anyone else read it this way?

**Score:** 31
**Body:** I recently watched *The Holdovers* and what struck me most wasn’t its supposed “warmth,” but its posture of restraint.

It felt like a film that behaves in a **Stoic** way — not because it talks about Stoicism, but because it practices it formally:

restraint instead of catharsis, duration instead of payoff, acceptance instead of resolution. The camera doesn’t guide emotion, the edit doesn’t accelerate it, the music doesn’t console.

Hunham’s arc doesn’t read to me as redemption so much as **alignment**: he accepts loss without trying to compensate for it. Change remains limited, incomplete — and that seems to be the point.

I was curious whether others read the film along these lines: less as a comfort movie or nostalgic exercise, and more as a deliberate act of **narrative and moral restraint**.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'm with you. It's a movie about approaching things logically, even when faced with the indifferent and illogical universe.

I don't think Hunham has much of an arc, and that he was always the selfless professor who would've done what's best for his students no matter what the cost.

The movie is best summed up by the phrase "It is what it is." 

Memory matters. Whatever you can squeeze out of the present matters. But losses are more or less permanent.

I say 'more or less' because by the end, Hunham is the closest thing to a father figure that the boy has had in his life. So even though his father is gone, that same nurturing spirit does exist in others.
  - I’d argue Hunham does have an arc — just not a transformational one: it’s an ethical realignment, where the principle remains and the cost shifts onto him. As Marcus Aurelius puts it: Recte fac, et quidquid evenit accipe (Do what is right, and accept whatever follows).
2. I really like what you described in your third paragraph. I felt that way while watching it as well, while the themes are clear in the film, I never felt like it held my hand to invoke the emotion, there's not the huge swelling emotional moments, but the quieter, more realistic beats between, especially the handshake scene. There is a huge payoff there, but not in the ways that we're typically used to in a lot of stories. And that's not to discredit those moments in other stories, but more to say that it really fits the tone and narrative set by The Holdovers as it's own language to portray growth and like you said, acceptance over resolution. 

As in, life moves forward, maybe not completely changed in every fundamental way, but certainly with a greater understanding and more emotional equipment to handle the future for our characters, all through what they've taught each other in their own ways.
3. i don’t think this is on topic but i have to say i completely agree on your point that hunham doesn’t have a redemption arc- the film fed us information to the point that we reached an understanding of who he was as a person that brought us closer to him. i’ve shown people this film countless times and when anyone says much of a bastard he is i just say “wait and give him a chance” and i just love that it’s written that way, rather than forcing an heavy handed emotional lesson on the character
4. **Edit / clarification:** Sorry — I removed the previous version because it leaned too much on the article itself and not enough on the film, which wasn’t what I wanted for this discussion.

If anyone is interested in the longer piece, I’m happy to share it privately or post the link here. But what I’m really curious about is whether this reading of the film resonates with others.

## 3. No Other Choice by Park Chan-wook

**Score:** 10
**Body:** No Other Choice (2025) is a witty, cool, and bleak reality of life. It starts with an upper middle-class family, which we all dream of getting in one way or another, inundated with “extra” kindness, “extra” bedsheets, “extra” flowers, and every “extra” one can assume, as well as “enough” or “rightfully accurate” life choices—but it all feels choreographed.

It’s incredible to watch all the favourite artists from K-drama, from Lee Byung-hun to Yeom Hye-ran, so it’s really worth a while.

What really intrigued me is the last scene: the girl playing the cello because she had no choice but to create and then remember those patterns. In my view, it felt like coding to me, or some kind of pattern on which the modern world is based. No more choice—just repeated patterns to walk on. It was not stupid, but intellectually depressive.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. Roadgames (1981) Paranoia, perception, and the birth of moving liminal space

**Score:** 5
**Body:** [Hitchcock on Wheels](https://open.substack.com/pub/horscadrecinema/p/roadgames-1981?utm_campaign=post-expanded-share&utm_medium=post%20viewer)

Roadgames is not a film about a killer on the highway as it seems first, it is a film about a consciousness unravelling across the Australian interior, where distance becomes surveillance and movement becomes entrapment. What begins as a playful road movie with Hitchcockian overtones gradually mutates into a metaphysical chamber piece stretched across asphalt. The desert in *Wake in Fright* held you in one location and boiled you alive psychologically; in *Roadgames*, the entire country becomes a corridor you cannot exit, a linear threshold from which there is no deviation. There is no town to escape from, only an ever-receding horizon that mirrors the expanding paranoia of a mind watching itself.

Pat Quid (Stacy Keach) is not a hero. He is a man alone in motion, and motion here is not freedom, it is exposure. We meet him through observation: watching other people through his windshield, playing games of deduction to pass the time. But like all acts of observation in cinema, the gaze becomes reciprocal. The more he watches, the more the road watches him. The premise echoes *Rear Window*, yes, but where Hitchcock’s voyeur is confined in place, Quid’s imprisonment is movement itself. His truck cab is not a refuge. It is a pulpit of paranoia, a stage from which he narrates a world that starts narrating him back.

Franklin understood what few directors dared to see in the outback: that its scale was not liberating, but hallucinatory. The Australian road is not a setting but a psychic mechanism. The more you drive, the more it folds in on itself. Days become interchangeable. Space loses distinction. The horizon ceases to recede and begins to revolve, like a mirage held perfectly still. *Roadgames* traps its characters in a continuity loop disguised as progress.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!1Zrd!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3c2c486e-db7e-43ab-bcf5-75ff43ae0ab1_1920x834.png)

The opening shot says everything: a truck slicing through the dawn, followed by tiny motel windows glowing like eyes. The film declares its grammar at once. Machines and architecture gaze back. Isolation is not absence but awareness. Even the headlights in the night penetrates, instead of illuminating the road. They become beams probing Quid’s psyche, turning him from observer into suspect. The windshield, the rearview mirror, the chrome; all are surfaces of reflection and return. The landscape has no need to speak; it watches, and that is enough.

Hitch, played by Jamie Lee Curtis, enters the narrative as contagion. Her very name is a wink, although the joke curdles quickly. She listens, then begins to speak in the same speculative language Quid uses to occupy the void. When she starts guessing what the other drivers are thinking, the infection is complete. Paranoia, once spoken aloud, becomes shared. Franklin makes this shift so fluidly that the viewer barely notices it happen: the narrative voice doubles itself, self-awareness becomes duet, then echo, then feedback. What Quid invents to survive solitude turns into a trap of language.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!gXp4!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7f671b41-ee17-4a6a-a1af-efdb46532199_1920x821.png)

This is a story about the mind’s desperate need to connect dots even when they don’t exist. Quid creates stories not because he wants truth, but because he cannot bear randomness. The road becomes his canvas, each passing car a possible clue, every motel light a potential omen. The killer may be real or imagined; it does not matter. What matters is that the act of storytelling replaces existence. Better to live inside a narrative of danger than inside a silence that refuses meaning. It is a logic that feels disturbingly modern, decades ahead of its time. Today, when every feed turns curiosity into conspiracy, *Roadgames* reads like prophecy. The highway becomes a data stream, paranoia the price of connection.

Franklin was too intelligent to confine fear to psychology. He knew that the non-human world carries its own vocabulary of dread. The film’s imagery of animals dead meat, scavenging birds, a dingo carcass on the roadside is not decorative reads as witnesses. The animal gaze, silent and unjudging, reflects the moral corrosion of human routine. Like the kangaroos in *Wake in Fright* or the birds in *Picnic at Hanging Rock*, they look without intervention. They endure. They remind us that we are the aberration in the landscape, not its center.

There is a strange realism beneath the hallucination. *Roadgames* draws its pulse from fragments of true history, as *Psycho* did from Ed Gein. Franklin blurs myth and memory so that the boundaries between cinematic paranoia and lived Australian fear dissolve. The camp sequence filmed near a ruined telegraph station once erased by a plague of rabbits, feels less like trivia than haunting: an echo of how the country devours its own attempts at permanence. What begins as realism metastasizes into omen. The road, the ruins triggers the repetition that all are symptoms of a place where narrative and geography share the same bloodstream.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!i4jO!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F82cb7d22-a26f-4d83-adf3-773aed1947bd_1920x810.png)

Franklin’s film lives between genres the way its characters live between states. It belongs to the Ozploitation lineage only insofar as it uses its tools, minimal budgets, maximal tension, the desert as theatre to reach metaphysical territory. Like *Duel* or *Sorcerer*, *Roadgames* understands that pursuit is not suspense but revelation. The chase is a method for stripping the self bare. Franklin studied Hitchcock, admired Carpenter, and in the process built a bridge between them. If *Wake in Fright* was the still life of Australian anxiety, *Roadgames* is its motion study.

Cinematically, it is exquisite in its restraint. Vincent Monton’s photography makes asphalt shimmer like liquid, headlights burn like giallo wounds. The frame hums with electrical paranoia, machines, glass, animal cries, the metallic rhythm of wheels devouring distance. The result is hypnosis. There are no jump scares because there is nowhere to jump. Only the hum, the road, and the sense that time itself is beginning to watch.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!dec1!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4f3aa3e6-cd49-4a30-a4ef-f4aae89efe43_1920x953.png)

**Spectre of Success**

Director: Richard Franklin  
Director of Photography: Vincent Monton  
Starring: Stacy Keach, Jamie Lee Curtis  
Budget: \~1.75 million AUD  
Box Office: Underperformed in Australia; modest cult acclaim overseas  
International Reception: Praised by John Carpenter, Quentin Tarantino; became a quiet influence on paranoia-in-transit cinema  
Legacy: Frequently cited as Australia’s greatest road thriller; precursor to *The Hitcher*, *Duel*, and the anxiety-driven cinema of surveillance

The film’s performance mirrored its themes, underseen, undervalued, endlessly moving. A commercial misfire that refused to vanish, it survived through rumor, home video, late-night broadcasts, and the fidelity of directors who saw in it something essential. Its afterlife is quieter than *Wake in Fright*’s resurrection, but its influence runs just as deep, winding through decades of cinema that equate travel with psychic exposure.

**Exit Without Arrival**

*Roadgames* is a film about the impossibility of arriving anywhere unchanged. The killer may be caught. But what dragged with him is the truth that the journey has already done its work. Quid leaves the road, but the road does not leave him. Like John Grant stepping out of the Yabba, he is no longer a man with an itinerary, he is a man who has seen how thin the line is between witness and participant, between story and delusion.

What remains after the credits is not fear, but hum. The sound of tires on heat-softened asphalt, the faint echo of a CB radio, the lingering question of whether the gaze that followed you belonged to someone else or to the country itself. The road becomes a verdict.

  
[Hors Cadre](https://horscadrecinema.substack.com/)

## 5. Hollywood vs Egypt: How the Devil Changed His Language

**Score:** 3
**Body:** Hollywood vs Egypt: How the Devil Changed His Language



In Hollywood’s The Devil and Daniel Webster (1941) (or All That Money Can Buy), Evil wears a suit. The Devil debates, bargains, and hides behind legality.



Four years later, Egyptian cinema responded with Safir Gahannam (1945)—The Ambassador of Hell. It is Not a remake in the mechanical sense, but a cultural translation. 



The American Devil argues his case in a courtroom shaped by Protestant morality and legal symbolism. The Egyptian Devil walks among people, testing desire, weakness, and self-deception within a moral universe shaped by Islamic faith, fate, and inner struggle.
